nativeRange.habitat: Bucephalandra Artemis is a named cultivar of a genus endemic to the island of Borneo. Wild Bucephalandra grow as rheophytes — plants adapted to fast-moving water — on rocks and submerged wood along fast-flowing rainforest streams and riverbanks.

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood or other porous hardscape for attaching the rhizome
gentle to moderate flow across the rhizome, mimicking rheophyte stream conditions
shaded to moderately lit foreground or midground positions
established, stable tank — avoid newly cycled systems

goodWith: Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp that graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and aquatic mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) likely to dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Bucephalandra Artemis is a slow-growing epiphytic cultivar from Borneo with small, elongated, slightly wavy green leaves that develop a subtle blue, purple, or metallic-silver iridescence under moderate to brighter lighting. Like all Bucephalandra, it is attached to hardscape — never buried in substrate.
careNotes:
Bucephalandra Artemis is a cultivar of the Borneo-endemic aroid genus Bucephalandra (family Araceae), and its general care follows the same principles as the genus — see [fw-plant-016] for shared baseline husbandry. Like all Bucephalandra, it is a small rheophytic epiphyte whose creeping rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which leaves and roots emerge) is attached to hardscape such as lava rock, driftwood, or slate using cyanoacrylate glue gel, thread, or fishing line, and left fully exposed to the water column. **DO NOT BURY THE RHIZOME** — burying it is generally reported to cause rot.

Artemis produces small, elongated, slightly wavy leaves arranged in a characteristic palm-tree-like pattern along the rhizome, which distinguishes it from broader-leaved cultivars such as 'Brownie' or 'Kedagang'. Coloration is predominantly vibrant to dark green, with subtle hints of blue, purple, or metallic silver that come out most strongly under moderate to higher lighting — a structural-color effect typical of the genus.

Lighting is flexible: low to medium light supports healthy, if slow, growth, while brighter light enhances the iridescent coloration. That said, brighter conditions on a slow-growing plant tend to invite algae on the leaves unless paired with CO2 supplementation. CO2 is optional but visibly improves both growth rate and color. Water parameters of 21–28 °C and pH 6.0–7.5 reflect the genus-level consensus and suit this cultivar well.

Growth is very slow — Artemis is considered especially slow even by Bucephalandra standards, which is why it is typically sold in small portions of around 1–2 inches. Nutrients are absorbed from the water column rather than the substrate, so light liquid fertilization is sufficient and the choice of substrate is irrelevant.

Like other Bucephalandra, Artemis may experience 'buce melt' when first transitioned from emersed (grown above water) to submersed growth, or after a sharp change in temperature, pH, or TDS (total dissolved solids). As long as the rhizome itself stays firm, new submersed-form leaves typically regrow over the following weeks. Artemis is best introduced into established, stable tanks rather than newly cycled systems.
breedingNotes: Bucephalandra Artemis is propagated in the hobby by rhizome division. Once the plant is established and actively producing new leaves and roots, the rhizome can be cut at a natural bend using clean, sharp scissors, ensuring each division retains several leaves and some roots. The new piece is then reattached to its own piece of hardscape. Side shoots that the rhizome produces over time provide convenient, ready-made division points. Sexual reproduction through the genus' spathe and spadix inflorescence is occasionally observed on emersed plants but is not a practical method in the aquarium.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Documents Borneo riverbank origin, low-to-moderate lighting (brighter enhances color), optional CO2, temperature 70-82°F (21-28°C), slow epiphyte growth, sold in 1-2 inch portions, foreground-to-midground placement, propagation by rhizome division with side shoots, easy care, rhizome must never be buried, vibrant green slightly wavy leaves with subtle blue/purple hints under good lighting.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked Borneo endemism, low-to-medium lighting (metallic silver sheen under brighter light), CO2 optional but recommended for faster growth, pH 6.0-8.0, slow growth, small elongated wavy leaves in a palm tree-like formation, dark green with iridescent highlights, foreground-to-midground placement, propagation by rhizome division with sharp scissors, easy care, epiphyte (attach to rock/wood, remove rockwool, never bury rhizome), tolerates submersed or emersed growth, sensitive to drastic parameter changes.
